result:
I keep thinking about the moment just before a story begins—those milliseconds when the page is still blank, the throat still closed, the shutter still shut.  It’s not silence; it’s a supersaturated quiet, like the pause between lightning and thunder when you’re not sure how far the storm has traveled to find you.  Inside that pause lives every possible first sentence, every possible life you might step into, every possible you.  Most of us rush through it the way we rush through the first sip of coffee, scalding our tongues and missing the notes of cedar or blueberry or whatever the bag claims.  But the moment doesn’t care if we notice; it keeps flowering outward, an invisible baobab whose roots are made of whispers we haven’t yet whispered.

Yesterday I watched a toddler discover the shadow of her own hand on a supermarket wall.  She spread her fingers, squinted, folded the hand into a beak, then a fin, then a zero.  Each shape arrived as a new planet, and she named them with a consonant—”K!” “Ba!” “Mm!”—the way the first humans must have named constellations they still hadn’t decided were permanent.  Her mother, impatient, tugged her toward the cereal aisle, and the planets were abolished.  But I kept seeing them, ghosted against the fluorescent-lit drywall, still orbiting the girl who was now kicking her legs from the cart-seat and howling for frosted flakes.  I wanted to tell the mother: Wait, let her stay exiled in that tiny eternity, because later—when report cards, rent, and rote despair arrive—she’ll need the memory of a self large enough to swallow the sun.  I didn’t say anything.  Advice is just another shadow that pretends it’s solid.

Instead I came home and opened every window, even though the temperature had dropped to the sort of cold that makes bike spokes ping like out-of-tune xylophones.  I let the rooms fill with that metallic night air, hoping it might carry in some unused syllables.  Instead it carried the neighbor’s piano, a hesitant nocturne I’d never heard.  The pianist hit the same wrong note three times, backed up, tried again, hit it harder, then softer, then finally skirted it entirely by rewriting the phrase downward a whole step, as if the composition itself had flinched.  The flaw became a door.  I stood in my kitchen, barefoot on the linoleum time-machine, listening to a stranger invent a detour that would thereafter always feel destined.  I thought: This is what revision is— not erasing the crack but making the crack the reason the vase can now sing.

After the music stopped, the apartment felt too small, so I walked to the river that isn’t really a river anymore, just a slow rumor of water engineered into concrete shoulders.  The city has planted LED lilies that bloom in alternating colors no pollinator recognizes.  They look like props from a canceled space opera, and yet—because they move slightly with the wind—they still register as alive.  We’ll take anything that sways: plastic bags, prayer flags, the ponytail of a woman jogging past.  We’ll call it companionship, same way we call the internet a village, same way we call a ghost a memory who refuses to sit down.  I knelt, touched the fake water, and felt the real chill.  Something bit me: not a mosquito (too cold) but the year itself, trying to draw a drop of blood as evidence that I was still porous.  I let it.

On the walk back I passed the shuttered optometry shop where my father once had his glasses fixed the month before he died.  The store is gone; the sign remains, sun-faded until the serif letters resemble eyelashes fallen onto a cheek.  I still expect to see his reflection inside that ghost sign, looking out, wondering why I haven’t brought him the car yet, why I haven’t learned to drive stick, why time insists on departing in the very vehicle we haven’t mastered.  Instead I see my own face, older than his final age, which is the first miracle any child survives: outliving the measurement of a parent’s years.  The second miracle is returning home with that uncomfortable surplus of calendar and finding the kettle still fits your palm, the quilt still remembers your knees, the notebook still opens to the same blank baobab.

So I sit now in the hush before the kettle clicks, before the day chooses its genre—tragedy, farce, or the most precarious of all: dull.  I blow on the surface of that pre-sentence quiet and watch ripples race outward, each one a possible beginning:  In the year all passwords turned to ash…  or  She collected discarded left shoes the way sailors once collected mermaids…  or  On the far side of the planet, a single phoneme vanished from every language at once…  Pick one, the page hisses, but I can’t; I love the flock too much to sacrifice any individual.  Instead I let them graze, these ghost sentences, these lantern ideas, until the kettle finally exhales its small steampunk death and the room fills with the smell of futures that have decided, without consulting me, to arrive as coffee.

I drink, I burn my tongue, I proceed.

prompt: Write freely about whatever you want.
